Buffer Areas Available to the Connector

Buffer Areas Available to the Connector

The following table shows the major buffer areas used to exchange data between the DBMS, the connector, and Uniface.

Table 1. Major Buffers used to Exchange Data
Informal buffer name Size Start address available in Purpose
General I/O buffer 8 KB

uctrl->iobuf

Exchange records with the data manipulation connector requests
SQL buffer 10 KB

uctrl->sqlbuf

Build statements in the native query language of the DBMS.
Connector workspace Defined by connector

uctrl->udrv

Store information needed for connector requests that apply to the same table
CAUTION: Be aware that these buffers can be temporarily stored in Uniface virtual memory and relocated between calls to the connector.

The connector must not assume that the buffer is located at the same start address for every connector request. It must obtain the start addresses of the buffers from the uctrl structure with every call to the connector.

This also implies that pointers into the buffers may not be kept across more than one connector request. If a data structure, such as a linked list, is implemented in the connector workspace, it must be based on offsets rather than on pointers.

General I/O Buffer

The general I/O buffer is primarily used to exchange records with the data manipulation connector requests. The size of the general I/O buffer is the same as the Uniface maximum record size, which is 8192 bytes for entities that have variable length fields.

The start positions of the fields in the record are available in the field list. For more information, see ufldlst.

SQL Buffer

The SQL buffer is available to build statements in the native query language of the DBMS. Uniface may read and print the contents of the SQL buffer after a call to the connector, for example, to display the SQL statements sent to the DBMS in the message frame.

If the DBMS supports a query language, every statement sent to it on executing a connector request, must be present as a null-terminated ASCII string in the SQL buffer when the connector returns to Uniface. If the DBMS does not support a query language, the first character in the SQL buffer must be zero when the connector returns to Uniface. Zero is its initial value.

Part of the SQL buffer is also used by the ubldprm service function to store the concatenated primary key.

The SQL buffer can also be used to return additional connector messages to Uniface. The messages will be displayed in the message frame together with possible SQL statements generated by the connector.

Connector Workspace

The connector can request a workspace in the Info call. A workspace is associated with an open table. If a workspace is requested, there is exactly one workspace for every open table. This is used to store information needed for connector requests that apply to the same table. With a record-level DBMS, it can be used to store the file descriptor that is obtained in the Open request. With a relational DBMS, it can be used to store a set of cursors.

The connector workspace is not available with some connector requests. See uctrl.

The connector can determine the size of the workspace. See uinfo.

Related Links
  • ufldlst

    Describes the fields in a table.

  • ubldprm

    ubldprm builds the concatenated primary key for a record.
